pred_reason: The SWC-105 label is appropriate because the contract has a payable fallback function without any withdrawal mechanism. This matches the classic SWC-105 pattern where ether sent to the contract becomes permanently locked. The finding confirms this vulnerability exists in the fallback function. Evidence: Payable fallback function without withdrawal mechanism (line 87-92); Static analysis detection of locked-ether vulnerability; Impact: Permanent loss of any ether sent to contract

findings.Description: This issue appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results. The contract has a payable fallback function but lacks any withdrawal mechanism, meaning ether sent to the contract becomes permanently locked.
findings.Impact: Any ether sent to the contract address (via fallback function or forced send) cannot be recovered, resulting in permanent loss of funds.
findings.Location: Static analysis finding: 'locked-ether' detector; Code location: fallback function (line 87-92)

findings.Description: This issue appears in the smart contract code only. The contract uses Solidity 0.4.15 which lacks many modern security features and contains known vulnerabilities.
findings.Impact: Increased risk of compiler-level vulnerabilities, missing security features, and potential compatibility issues with modern tooling and infrastructure.

findings.Issue: Potential Gas Limit Vulnerability in Loops
findings.Description: This issue appears in the smart contract code only. Multiple functions use unbounded loops over owner arrays which could exceed gas limits if the owner count grows too large.
findings.Impact: Transactions may fail if gas requirements exceed block gas limit, potentially making contract operations unusable with large owner sets.
findings.Location: Functions: isConfirmed(), getConfirmationCount(), getConfirmations(), getTransactionIds(), removeOwner()

findings.Issue: Missing Input Validation in External Call
findings.Description: This issue appears in the smart contract code only. The external_call function does not validate that the destination address contains code before making the call.
findings.Impact: Transactions may succeed without actually executing any code, potentially leading to false positive execution confirmations.
